# Clinical Substrate: transitions-first-two-weeks

> Clinical-depth pass for Synthesis integration. Not the final module.
> Voice target: exhausted family caregiver, phone, late at night.
> Canonical headings for a transitions (non-behavioral) module.

---

## What this is

Your family member has been diagnosed with dementia. The appointment is over, and you are carrying a word you did not choose. This module covers the first two weeks — what is normal, what can wait, and the small number of things worth doing now.

How the diagnosis was made may look different from what you expected. In 2025–2026, many clinicians use a blood test — measuring a protein called phosphorylated tau — as part of the diagnostic workup [1][2][3]. The Alzheimer's Association published its first clinical guideline on these blood-based biomarkers in July 2025, reporting that the tests agree with the traditional PET brain scan 91.7% of the time when positive and 97.3% of the time when negative [1]. Your doctor may have used one of these newer blood tests, a PET scan, a lumbar puncture, or a combination. The method does not change what to do this week — and a separate module covers the diagnostic pathway in depth.

## Why it matters now

The first two weeks are when caregivers are most likely to make decisions they later regret — selling a house, quitting a job, telling everyone at once — or to freeze and do nothing, including things that genuinely matter [5][7]. The 2024 Lancet Commission on dementia noted that timely support in the period immediately following diagnosis reduces long-term caregiver distress and improves outcomes for both the person with dementia and the family [7]. This is not a crisis. It is a transition, and transitions have a shape.

## What to do this week

Pick one or two of these. Not all of them.

**Write down what the clinician said** while you still remember the exact words: the type of dementia named (or "not yet specified"), any medications started or considered, what tests are still pending, and when the follow-up appointment is [5]. If you did not get clear answers on those four things, send a short message to the clinic asking. You are allowed to ask twice.

**Tell one person.** Not the whole family, not a group text. One person you trust who can sit with the news without making it about themselves. Saying it out loud to one human face changes something [6]. If there is no one safe in your life right now, a caregiver helpline counts — the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 Helpline (1-800-272-3900) is staffed by licensed clinicians [5].

**Ask the clinic if they participate in the GUIDE program.** The CMS GUIDE Model (Guiding an Improved Dementia Experience) went live at 390 organizations in July 2025 [4]. If your clinic participates, you may be eligible for care navigation, caregiver education, and respite support — reimbursed up to roughly $2,500 per year [4]. One phone call or portal message to the clinic can tell you.

**Locate key documents.** Find the most recent driver's license renewal, insurance card, and any existing power of attorney or healthcare proxy. You do not need to act on them this week. You just need to know where they are. If they do not exist yet, that is a month-two task, not a week-one task [5].

**Know that new treatment options may come up.** Your clinician may mention disease-modifying therapies that did not exist a few years ago [7]. That is a month-one conversation between you, your family member, and the clinical team — not a week-one decision. It can wait for the follow-up appointment.

**Eat something. Sleep, if you can.** The body keeps the score, and you are going to need it [6].

## When to call sooner

Some things in the first two weeks warrant a call back to the clinician — do not wait for the next appointment.

- **Sudden increased confusion over hours or days.** A noticeable jump in confusion — beyond what was present at diagnosis — can signal delirium from an infection (urinary tract infections are common), a medication interaction, or another treatable cause [7]. Delirium is not the dementia getting worse overnight; it is a medical emergency layered on top, and it is treatable when caught early.
- **New medication side effects.** If a medication was started at the diagnostic visit and your family member develops nausea, dizziness, falls, new agitation, or anything that feels wrong — call [5][6].
- **Safety concerns today.** If driving feels unsafe right now and you do not know how to stop it, call the clinic; they have a protocol for this [5]. If your family member is wandering, leaving the stove on, or putting themselves or others at risk in a way that cannot wait, call.
- **Talk of self-harm or harm to others.** Call the clinic, or contact emergency services or a crisis line. That is not an overreaction [5][6].

If **you** are the one not okay — if you are having thoughts of hurting yourself, or you cannot keep yourself or your family member safe — contact emergency services or the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. That is the system working as intended.

## How to care for yourself in this season

This is grief, even though no one has died. The person you love is still here, and a particular version of the future is not. Both of those are real at the same time, and that doubleness is what makes the early weeks so disorienting [7]. You do not have to name it as grief out loud yet if that word feels too big. You can call it tired. You can call it strange. You can call it nothing.

The 2024 Lancet Commission identified caregiver psychological distress in the first months after diagnosis as a predictor of longer-term burnout and noted that even minimal early intervention — a single counseling session, a peer-support contact, a structured information packet — reduced that trajectory [7]. You do not need to sign up for a support group tonight. But know that the Alzheimer's Association [5] and the National Institute on Aging [6] both maintain caregiver resource centers that are free, available now, and designed for exactly this moment.

What helps in the first two weeks is almost embarrassingly simple. Lower the bar. Cancel one thing. Let the house be messier. Say no to the dinner. Walk around the block. Cry in the car. Do not make any decisions you would not make on a regular Tuesday. The ground will steady, slowly, and when it does you will be able to think more clearly than you can right now.

---

## Clinical notes for review

1. Blood-biomarker paragraph kept to one paragraph per brief risk #1 (over-medicalizing). Framing is descriptive ("your doctor may have used"), not prescriptive, per brief risk #5.
2. DMTs mentioned in one sentence in action items. No drug names, no dosing, no mechanism. Points to month-one / follow-up conversation.
3. GUIDE Model dollar figure (~$2,500/year) sourced from CMS GUIDE Model overview. Exact amount varies by region and service mix; "roughly" qualifier used.
4. Delirium call-back item emphasizes treatable causes to prevent catastrophic misattribution ("the dementia is getting worse fast").
5. pTau217 and pTau181 concordance figures (91.7% / 97.3%) from AAIC 2025 guideline. These are population-level concordance with amyloid PET, not individual-level sensitivity/specificity — wording kept appropriately general.
